narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, you've put real effort into your law school journey, including choosing full-time studies ahead. The admissions model we use looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated your risk of failure at 46.1%—high enough that your application was rejected to help ensure strong outcomes for our program. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which is below the typical student's 5.5-ish level and signaled to the model a tougher path to bar success compared to higher performers. On a brighter note, your middle family income actually worked in your favor, pushing the odds toward passing more than average cases do, as it aligns with many successful students' profiles. Your LSAT score of 32 also helped nudge things positively, even though it's under the usual 36.7 benchmark, showing some resilience in standardized testing. Your Year 1 decile of 3 and undergraduate GPA of 2.8 had smaller mixed influences, with the former slightly helping and the latter tilting a bit toward risk, but neither dominated the decision. Overall, your profile stands out as lower than typical in key academic deciles and scores, which outweighed the supportive elements like your full-time commitment that matches 93% of students. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model highlighting where past data shows the steepest hurdles for bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your upper-year grades if possible, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your full-time dedication and background. You could also seek bar prep resources early or connect with alumni who've overcome similar stats. We're rooting for you to build on these insights and reapply stronger—law needs determined voices like yours. Keep pushing ahead.
